figures.caption: Figure 1: Modeled measurement setup with two FPA-FTIR measuring stations (green-yellow dots, left), FPA-FTIR hyperspectral imaging measurement device in another campaign [2](top middle), synthetic signal (extracted contour) representing gas tomography result in the modeled setup (right)
Figure 2: Shows the test area Ω (left) and the estimated wind field (right)

Figure 3: Numerical simulation of contamination dispersion at the beginning of the release (𝑡 = 0.0 s, top left), at the measurement time (𝑡 = 5.0 s, top right) , after 𝑡 = 40.0 s (bottom left) and at the final simulation time (𝑡 = 100.0 s, bottom right).

Figure 4: Shows the contaminant after 5.0 s seconds and the contour Γ5.0 s = {𝑥 ∈ Ω|𝑢true(𝑡, 𝑥) = 10−3} captured by the measurement system (yellow ellipse, left) and the solution q of the adjoint problem () for 𝑐 = 10−3 at 𝑡 = 𝑡obs, computed using the inverted wind field (right).
Figure 5: Predicted raw sources 𝜆𝑖 𝛿𝑥𝑖 (left) and derived source 𝜇post (red dot) near ground truth source center (black cross, right)
Figure 6: Reconstruction of the contaminant concentration at measurement time 5.0 s; compare to Figure 4 and the absolute difference 𝑢true(5.0 s, ⋅) − 𝐹 (𝜇)(5.0 s, ⋅)
Figure 7: Reconstruction of the contaminant concentration at measurement time 100.0 s; compare to Figure 3 and the absolute difference 𝑢true(100.0 s, ⋅) − 𝐹 (𝜇)(5.0 s, ⋅)
Table 1: Reconstruction of the source location 𝑥𝑠 = [−241,−41] at the test site, cf. Figure 5

Figure 8: Shows the recorded values (black dots) of two FPA-FTIR hyperspectral imaging devices (green-yellow dots) and the extracted contour (pink) in a three-dimensional environment (left) and a synthetic signal representing the information gained from a lidar measurement in the modeled setup (right)
